title: Installation
description: How to install ctrodb
content:
Use Node.js version 20+ for Node environments
 ctrodb works in any modern browser and Node.js 20+
 TypeScript declarations are bundled — no need for @types/ctrodb
 React bindings are included via ctrodb/react — no additional package required

title: Installation
description: How to install ctrodb
content: After loading from CDN, access the library globally using CtroDB.

title: Installation
description: How to install ctrodb
content: If this runs successfully, ctrodb is ready to use.

description: Get up and running with ctrodb in under a minute
section: Get up and running with ctrodb in under a minute
content: Create a database, define a schema, and start reading and writing data.

title: Quick Start
description: Get up and running with ctrodb in under a minute
content: This initializes the storage adapter. By default it picks IndexedDB in browsers and memory in Node.

title: Quick Start
description: Get up and running with ctrodb in under a minute
content: Read the Database docs for details.

title: CDN Usage
description: Use ctrodb directly in the browser via script tag
content:
All features work from CDN
IndexedDB adapter works in all modern browsers
Memory adapter works everywhere
The full bundle is ~25 KB gzip

See the CDN example for a complete todo app.

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
section: The main entry point for ctrodb
content: Database is the top-level object. It manages the connection, schema, collections, and plugins.

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
content:
| Option | Type | Default | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| name | string | "ctrodb" | Database name |
| adapter | "indexeddb" \| "memory" \| StorageAdapter | auto | Storage backend |
| schema | SchemaConfig | — | Schema definition |
| plugins | CtrochemicalPlugin[] | — | Plugin list |
| logLevel | "debug" \| "info" \| "warn" \| "error" \| "silent" | — | Log level |

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
content:
connect() initializes the adapter and triggers onDatabaseInit on plugins.
Call once before using collections. disconnect() clears all collection caches.

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
content: Collections are lazily created — first access initializes a new Collection.

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
content:
If the callback throws, all changes are rolled back.
The transaction context provides raw adapter CRUD methods.

title: Database
description: The main entry point for ctrodb
content:
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| type | "create" \| "update" \| "delete" | What happened |
| collection | string | Collection name |
| recordId | number \| string | Record ID |
| record | unknown | New value (create/update) |
| oldRecord | unknown | Previous value (update/delete) |

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
section: Define the shape of your data
content: A schema describes the collections, their fields, indexes, and relations.

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
content: version must be a positive integer. collections must have at least one entry.

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
content:
| Option | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| type | "string" \| "number" \| "boolean" \| "object" \| "array" | Data type |
| required | boolean | Throw if missing |
| default | unknown \| (() => unknown) | Applied on create if field is undefined |
| min | number | Minimum value (number fields) |
| max | number | Maximum value (number fields) |
| maxLength | number | Max string length |
| items | FieldDefinition | Schema for array items |
| unique | boolean | Enforce uniqueness via index |
| validate | "email" \| "url" \| RegExp \| ((value) => boolean) | Built-in validators |

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
content:
Indexes speed up queries using the operator ==, >, =, <=.
Unique indexes prevent duplicates.

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
content: Relations require the relations plugin to be active.

title: Schema
description: Define the shape of your data
content:
Built-in validation covers:
Required fields
Type checking (including array item types)
String min/max length
Number min/max
Email and URL format
Custom regex patterns
Unknown field rejection

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content: A Collection maps to a single table or object store. All operations are async.

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content:
Applies schema defaults
Validates against schema
Runs onBeforeCreate plugin hooks (can modify data)
Creates record in adapter
Runs onAfterCreate plugin hooks
Emits a change event

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content: get returns a single record or undefined. getAll returns every record.

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content:
Merges changes with existing data
Validates the merged result
Runs onBeforeUpdate / onAfterUpdate plugin hooks
Emits a change event

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content:
Runs onBeforeDelete / onAfterDelete plugin hooks
Emits a change event for each deleted record
delete throws if record does not exist
deleteMany silently skips missing IDs

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content: If data.id matches an existing record, updates it. Otherwise creates it.

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content: See Query Engine for details.

title: Collection
description: Read and write records
content:
Fires on every create, update, or delete in this collection.
Returns an unsubscribe function.

title: Model
description: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
section: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
content:
Every record returned from a collection is wrapped in a Model proxy.
You can access fields directly — no .get("field") calls needed.

title: Model
description: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
content:
The model is a Proxy that forwards property access to the underlying data.
Direct writes warn you to use .update() instead.

title: Model
description: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
content: update() and delete() call back into the collection's CRUD methods.

title: Model
description: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
content:
When the schema defines relations and the relations plugin is active,
the model auto-attaches lazy relation getters:

Each relation getter returns a QueryBuilder pre-filtered to match the relation.

title: Model
description: The record wrapper with methods and relation getters
content: The id is a getter on the data — not settable via the proxy.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content:
QueryBuilder provides a fluent API for reading data. It does not support joins —
use relations for related data.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content: Supported operators: ==, !=, >, =, <=

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content: Groups conditions with OR logic. Each orWhere block is one group.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content: Only one sort field per query. Passing multiple entries picks the first.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content: Use offset with limit for pagination. Offset skips records before slicing.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content:
Fuzzy substring search (case-insensitive). Requires the FTS plugin
for full-text indexing; falls back to String.includes() without it.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content: first() is equivalent to .limit(1).fetch() then returning results[0].

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content:
The QueryPlanner examines conditions and indexes to pick a strategy:

| Strategy | When | How |
|----------|------|-----|
| indexscan | Field has an index | Uses IndexedDB index with key range |
| idlookup | Querying by id field with == | Direct adapter lookup |
| full_scan | No index match | Iterates all records, filters in memory |

Sort, offset, and limit are applied in memory after fetching.

title: Query Engine
description: Filter, sort, and paginate records
content:
Uses JavaScript's  comparison. Mixed types compare via  rules —
numbers sort numerically, strings lexicographically.

title: Adapters Overview
description: Storage backends
content: Adapters handle persistence. ctrodb ships with two.

title: Adapters Overview
description: Storage backends
content:
| Adapter | Environment | Persistence |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| MemoryAdapter | Node, browser | Not persisted |
| IndexedDBAdapter | Browser only | Persists in IndexedDB |

title: Adapters Overview
description: Storage backends
content: You can also pick explicitly:

title: Adapters Overview
description: Storage backends
content:
Any object implementing the StorageAdapter interface works:

Full StorageAdapter interface in API reference.

title: Adapters Overview
description: Storage backends
content:
All adapters implement transaction(fn). If the callback throws, all changes
are rolled back:

title: MemoryAdapter
description: In-memory storage for Node and testing
section: In-memory storage for Node and testing
content: MemoryAdapter stores everything in a Map. Data is lost when the process exits.

title: MemoryAdapter
description: In-memory storage for Node and testing
content:
Each collection is a Map
IDs are auto-incrementing numbers (1, 2, 3, ...)
On schema connect, collections are pre-created
Transactions take a snapshot and restore on error

title: MemoryAdapter
description: In-memory storage for Node and testing
content:
Unit and integration tests
Server-side rendering
CLI tools and scripts
Demos and prototyping
Node.js environments

title: MemoryAdapter
description: In-memory storage for Node and testing
content: Auto-generated IDs are numbers. You can also provide string IDs:

title: MemoryAdapter
description: In-memory storage for Node and testing
content:
On transaction start, MemoryAdapter snapshots all maps.
If the transaction callback throws, the snapshot is restored.
Nested transactions are not supported.

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content: IndexedDBAdapter uses the browser's IndexedDB API. Data persists across sessions.

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content:
Each collection is an object store with id as keyPath
Auto-increment is handled by IndexedDB
Indexes are created during migration (onupgradeneeded)
A ctrodbmeta store holds schema version and metadata

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content:
When you change the schema version, IndexedDB fires onupgradeneeded.
The adapter creates new object stores and indexes:

New collections and indexes are added. Existing data is preserved.

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content:
All modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) support IndexedDB.
The adapter checks for window.indexedDB at runtime.

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content:
Uses IDBTransaction with readwrite mode across all object stores.
If the callback throws, tx.abort() is called.

title: IndexedDBAdapter
description: Persistent storage for browsers
content:
Not available in Node.js
IndexedDB size limits vary by browser (typically 50MB–unlimited)
Blocked on database version change (tab conflict)

title: Plugin Overview
description: Extend ctrodb with lifecycle hooks
content: Plugins hook into database and collection lifecycle events.

title: Plugin Overview
description: Extend ctrodb with lifecycle hooks
content:
For a create() call:
onBeforeCreate — plugins run in order, each can modify data
Schema defaults applied
Schema validation
Adapter creates record
onAfterCreate — plugins run in order

For update():
onBeforeUpdate — plugins can modify changes
Schema validation (merged with existing)
Adapter updates record
onAfterUpdate

For delete():
onBeforeDelete
Adapter deletes record
onAfterDelete

title: Plugin Overview
description: Extend ctrodb with lifecycle hooks
content:
For onBeforeCreate and onBeforeUpdate, the return value replaces the data
if not undefined. This lets plugins transform data before it is stored.

title: Full-Text Search
description: Token-based search across string fields
content: The FTS plugin indexes string fields for fast search queries.

title: Full-Text Search
description: Token-based search across string fields
content:
The plugin stores a token-to-document mapping in a ctrodbfts collection.

Indexing:
On create/update/delete, tokens are extracted from searchable fields
Each token maps to the set of document IDs containing it
Tokens are lowercased, deduplicated, and filtered for stop words

Searching:

Searches use AND logic — documents must match all query tokens.

title: Full-Text Search
description: Token-based search across string fields

title: Full-Text Search
description: Token-based search across string fields
content:
The tokenizer splits on non-alphanumeric characters and lowercases.
Duplicates are removed. Stop words are filtered.

title: Full-Text Search
description: Token-based search across string fields
content: For advanced use, you can access the indexer directly:

title: Relations
description: belongs_to, has_many, and has_one
content: The relations plugin enables eager loading and lazy relation getters.

title: Relations
description: belongs_to, has_many, and has_one
content:
| Type | Source | Target | Meaning |
|------|--------|--------|---------|
| belongsto | Post | User | Post has a userId pointing to User |
| hasmany | User | Post[] | User has many Posts via userId |
| has_one | User | Profile | User has one Profile via userId |

title: Relations
description: belongs_to, has_many, and has_one
content:
The plugin adds a .with() method to collections:

Chain multiple relations:

title: Relations
description: belongs_to, has_many, and has_one
content:
Without eager loading, models still have lazy getters:

Lazy getters return a QueryBuilder pre-filtered on the foreign key.
The belongsto getter filters targetcollection.where("id", "==", this.foreignKey).
The hasmany/hasone getter filters
target_collection.where("foreignKey", "==", this.id).

title: Validation
description: Custom validation rules for create and update
section: Custom validation rules for create and update
content: The validation plugin adds custom rules beyond schema-level validation.

title: Validation
description: Custom validation rules for create and update
content:
| Rule | Checks |
|------|--------|
| email | Validates email format on all string fields |
| url | Validates URL format on strings starting with http(s) |
| noEmptyStrings | Rejects empty or whitespace-only strings |

title: Validation
description: Custom validation rules for create and update
content: Return null for valid, a string error message for invalid.

title: Validation
description: Custom validation rules for create and update
content:
Runs on onBeforeCreate and onBeforeUpdate hooks
Validates all fields in the data object
Checks each field against every registered rule
Throws on first failure with all error messages joined

title: Custom Plugins
description: Write your own plugins
content: Any object matching the CtroDBPlugin interface can be a plugin.

title: Custom Plugins
description: Write your own plugins
content: Records every write operation in an audit_log collection:

title: Custom Plugins
description: Write your own plugins
content:
Transforms data before storage and after retrieval:

Note: since onBeforeUpdate returns data that is merged with the existing record,
be careful to return only the fields that should change.

title: useQuery
description: Reactive data fetching hook
content: useQuery fetches records and re-renders when data changes.

title: useQuery
description: Reactive data fetching hook
content: The deps array controls when the query function is re-evaluated.

title: useQuery
description: Reactive data fetching hook
content:
Runs the query on mount
Subscribes to change events for the collection
Re-runs the query on every change event
Cleans up subscription on unmount

title: useDoc
description: Fetch a single record by ID
section: Fetch a single record by ID
content: A convenience wrapper around useQuery for fetching one record.

title: useDoc
description: Fetch a single record by ID
content:
Returns undefined if the id is not provided (e.g., before data loads)
or if the record does not exist.

title: useMutation
description: Create, update, and delete records with loading/error state
content:
The error is set whenever a mutation throws. reset() clears both loading and error.
Re-throwing from the mutation call does not change error state — errors are already captured.

title: DatabaseProvider
description: React context provider for database access
section: React context provider for database access
content: DatabaseProvider makes the database instance available to all hooks in the tree.

title: DatabaseProvider
description: React context provider for database access
content: Providers can be nested. The innermost provider's database takes precedence.

title: DatabaseProvider
description: React context provider for database access
content:
Returns the database from context. Throws if no provider is found and no default
database is set.

title: DatabaseProvider
description: React context provider for database access
content:
DatabaseProvider calls setDefaultDatabase(db) and renders a context provider.
The useDatabase hook reads from context first, falling back to the default.

Throws SchemaError on invalid config (no version, no collections, empty collection,

title: Schema
description: API reference for the Schema class
content:
Throws ValidationError on:
Missing required fields
Type mismatch (including array item types)
String exceeds maxLength
Number below min or above max
Invalid email/URL format
Regex failure
Undefined fields (not in schema)

The id field is exempt from the undefined-field check.

Internal — models are created by Collection._toModel().

Model uses a Proxy to expose record fields as direct properties.

content: All record fields are accessible as properties:

content: Calls collection.delete(this.id).

content: Returns a plain copy of the data — no methods or proxy.

Auto-attached when the schema defines relations and the relations plugin is active.

Groups conditions with OR logic. Multiple where calls inside the same
orWhere are AND'd within the group.

title: Migration from Alpha
description: Changes from alpha releases to v1.0.0
content:
The stable release consolidates all alpha changes. The public API is stable
going forward.

title: Migration from Alpha
description: Changes from alpha releases to v1.0.0
content:
Version bumped to 1.0.0 (no breaking changes from alpha.6)
FTSIndexer.search() returns ID[] instead of Promise
Internal refactors only — no API changes

title: Migration from Alpha
description: Changes from alpha releases to v1.0.0
content:
onDatabaseInit now receives the Database instance instead of the adapter
createAdapter() signature changed — now takes an optional string type
QueryBuilder.search() moved from a terminal method to a chainable method
Plugin hooks now pass collection as first parameter for consistency
Validation plugin rules receive collection and field context

description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
section: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
content: The classic todo app is a good way to see how ctrodb's React hooks work in practice. This post walks through the full implementation.
author: ctrodb team

title: Building a Reactive Todo App with ctrodb and React
description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
content: Create the database with a schema:
author: ctrodb team

title: Building a Reactive Todo App with ctrodb and React
description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
content: useQuery fetches all todos and re-renders when anything changes in the collection:
author: ctrodb team

title: Building a Reactive Todo App with ctrodb and React
description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
content:
When create is called, ctrodb stores the record and emits a change event. useQuery subscribes to these events and re-fetches the data. The component re-renders with the new list — no manual state management needed.

The same flow works for updates and deletes. Every mutation triggers a re-render of every component querying the same collection.
author: ctrodb team

title: Building a Reactive Todo App with ctrodb and React
description: Step-by-step guide to building a real-time todo app using useQuery and useMutation
content: See the full example in the repo for a more complete version with search and relations.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
section: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content: Building ctrodb through six alpha releases to a stable v1.0.0 taught us a few things about TypeScript library design. Here is what we learned.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content:
The decision to have zero runtime dependencies shaped the entire architecture. Every feature had to be implemented from scratch or not included. This forced us to:
Write our own reactive signal implementation (35 lines)
Build a custom query planner instead of using a library
Keep the bundle small (~25 KB gzip)

Users appreciate not having to audit a deep dependency tree.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content:
Exporting proper types is more valuable than writing separate docs for every interface. The SchemaConfig type tells you exactly what a schema looks like. The StorageAdapter interface shows every method an adapter needs.

We export all public types alongside the classes. Users get autocomplete and type checking without switching between docs and code.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content:
The test suite grew from 30 tests in alpha.1 to 190 tests in v1.0.0. Every bug fix started with a failing test. The test coverage includes:
Schema validation edge cases
All query operators with various data types
Adapter-specific behavior
Plugin hook execution order
React hook rendering cycles

Integration tests catch issues that unit tests miss. A test that creates, queries, updates, and deletes records in sequence reveals timing and state management bugs.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content: Instead of building everything into the core, we extracted FTS search, relations, and validation into plugins. The plugin hook interface (before/after create/update/delete) has been stable since alpha.3. All three built-in plugins work together because they operate on different lifecycle hooks.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content:
We went from alpha.1 to alpha.6, then straight to v1.0.0. The jump to stable was possible because:
The API surface settled after alpha.3
Internal refactors did not change public types
Test coverage gave confidence

One thing we would do differently: ship the React bindings earlier.
author: ctrodb team

title: From Alpha to v1.0.0: Building a TypeScript Library
description: Lessons learned from building ctrodb — a zero-dependency client-side database
content: Now that v1.0.0 is stable, the focus is on documentation, examples, and community. The API is not expected to change in breaking ways.
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
section: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content: Full-text search is one of those features that feels simple until you implement it. This post explains how ctrodb's FTS plugin works under the hood.
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
Without an index, searching means iterating every record and running String.includes():

This works for small datasets. For hundreds or thousands of records, it gets slow.
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
ctrodb's FTS plugin takes a different approach. When a record is created or updated, it extracts tokens from searchable fields and stores a token-to-document mapping:

A search for "database schema" finds docs that have both tokens (AND logic).
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
The tokenizer:
Lowercases the input
Splits on non-alphanumeric characters
Removes duplicates
Filters out stop words (a, an, the, and, ...)
Returns unique tokens
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
The index lives in a ctrodbfts collection alongside your data. Each entry is:

This works with both MemoryAdapter and IndexedDBAdapter.
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
When you call .search("title", "database schema"):
The tokenizer splits the query into tokens: ["database", "schema"]
For each token, the indexer looks up the corresponding entry
Doc IDs that appear in ALL token entry sets are returned
These IDs are passed to the adapter for record retrieval
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
Indexing: O(tokens per record) — happens on every create/update/delete
Search: O(tokens in query) — constant time lookups per token
Storage: O(unique tokens x docs per token) — scales with vocabulary size
author: ctrodb team

title: Full-Text Search in the Browser
description: How ctrodb implements client-side full-text search without external services
content:
The FTS plugin works best for:
Searchable text fields (titles, descriptions, body content)
Datasets up to tens of thousands of records
Apps where exact substring matching is sufficient

It does not support fuzzy search, stemming, or relevance scoring. For those, consider a dedicated search service.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
section: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content: A good schema makes your app easier to build and maintain. Here are patterns that work well with ctrodb.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
ctrodb does not support nested document schemas. Each collection is a flat table. This is intentional — flat structures are easier to query, index, and maintain.

Instead of this:

Do this:

Use the relations plugin to connect them.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
Timestamps are common enough that defaults should be automatic:

There is no auto-update on mutation — you need to set updatedAt manually in your update calls.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
ctrodb does not have a native enum type. Use validate with a regex or function:

Or use the validation plugin for more complex rules.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
Set unique: true on an index to prevent duplicates:

The unique constraint is enforced by IndexedDB's index uniqueness.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
Mark string fields as searchable when you need full-text search:

Only string fields should be marked searchable. The FTS plugin ignores non-string values.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content:
The version field in SchemaConfig triggers IndexedDB migrations:

Start at version 1 and increment each time you change the schema shape. This ensures IndexedDB creates the correct object stores.
author: ctrodb team

title: Schema Design Patterns for Client-Side Apps
description: Practical patterns for structuring your client-side database schema
content: A client-side database works best with focused collections. A schema with 5-10 collections is easier to manage than one with 50. Split large schemas into separate database instances if needed.
author: ctrodb team

title: Why Client-Side Databases Matter
description: The shift towards local-first applications and what it means for developers
content: Users expect instant feedback. Click a button, see a result. No spinners, no loading states. But every network request adds 50-500ms of latency, and that adds up. A database that runs in the same process as your UI cuts that to zero.
author: ctrodb team

title: Why Client-Side Databases Matter
description: The shift towards local-first applications and what it means for developers
content: Mobile users go through tunnels. Desktop users lose WiFi. Progressive web apps need to work without a connection. A client-side database means your app keeps running when the network drops, and syncs when it comes back.
author: ctrodb team

title: Why Client-Side Databases Matter
description: The shift towards local-first applications and what it means for developers
content:
Existing client-side storage options each have trade-offs:
localStorage: synchronous, blocking, 5MB limit, strings only
IndexedDB raw: powerful but verbose, no schema, error-prone
SQLite (via WASM): heavy, complex, overkill for most apps
ctrodb: schema-driven, reactive, zero deps, ~25KB

ctrodb wraps IndexedDB (and memory storage) with a clean TypeScript API. You define your schema, get validation and indexes for free, and react to changes with a simple event system.
author: ctrodb team

title: Why Client-Side Databases Matter
description: The shift towards local-first applications and what it means for developers
content:
The most successful local-first apps follow a pattern:
Local database is the source of truth
UI renders from local data — no loading states for reads
Writes go to local DB first — instant feedback
Background sync pushes to server when connected

ctrodb handles steps 1-3. The sync layer is up to you.
author: ctrodb team

title: Why Client-Side Databases Matter
description: The shift towards local-first applications and what it means for developers
content: If you are building a new app, try starting with a client-side database and adding server sync later. You will be surprised how much you can build without a backend.
author: ctrodb team
